author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
title: The New Law Written Within.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Now, observe how consonant this diversity is with those words of the apostle which I quoted not long ago in another connection, and which I postponed for a more careful consideration afterwards: “Forasmuch,” says he, “as ye are manifestly declared to be the epistle of Christ ministered by us, written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God; not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: See how he shows that the one is written without man, that it may alarm him from without; the other within man himself, that it may justify him from within.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: He speaks of the “fleshy tables of the heart,” not of the carnal mind, but of a living agent possessing sensation, in comparison with a stone, which is senseless.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: The assertion which he subsequently makes,—that “the children of Israel could not look stedfastly on the end of the face of Moses,” and that he accordingly spoke to them through a veil,—signifies that the letter of the law justifies no man, but that rather a veil is placed on the reading of the Old Testament, until it shall be turned to Christ, and the veil be removed;—in other words, until it shall be turned to grace, and be understood that from Him accrues to us the justification, whereby we do what He commands.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: And He commands, in order that, because we lack in ourselves, we may flee to Him for refuge.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Accordingly, after most guardedly saying, “Such trust have we through Christ to God-ward,” the apostle immediately goes on to add the statement which underlies our subject, to prevent our confidence being attributed to any strength of our own.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: He says: “Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think anything as of ourselves; but our sufficiency is of God; who also hath made us fit to be ministers of the New Testament; not of the letter, but of the spirit: for the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life.”
